tasks.description: What was the total amount spent by VALERO ENERGY CORP/TX on share repurchases during the fourth quarter of 2024, and what was the average price paid per share?
tasks.gold_answer:
According to the 2025 10-K filing, VALERO ENERGY CORP/TX spent approximately $264 million on share repurchases in the fourth quarter of 2024.
The average price paid per share during that period was $127.96.
Calculation basis:
The table in the filing shows:
Total shares repurchased: 2,060,851
Average price per share: $127.96
Total amount calculation:
Total amount = Total shares repurchased × Average price per share
= 2,060,851 × $127.96
= $263,706,493.96
≈ $264 million
key_points: The total amount spent on share repurchases in Q4 2024 must be stated as approximately $264 million.
The average price paid per share in Q4 2024 must be stated as $127.96.
The answer must correctly specify that the data pertains to the fourth quarter of 2024.

PART II

ITEM 5. MARKET FOR REGISTRANT’S COMMON EQUITY, RELATED STOCKHOLDER MATTERS AND ISSUER PURCHASES OF EQUITY SECURITIES

Our common stock trades on the NYSE under the trading symbol “VLO.”

As of January 31, 2025, there were 4,196 holders of record of our common stock.

Dividends are considered quarterly by the Board, may be paid only when approved by the Board, and will depend on our financial condition, results of operations, cash flows, prospects, industry conditions, capital requirements, and other factors and restrictions our Board deems relevant. There can be no assurance that we will pay a dividend in the future at the rates we have paid historically, or at all.

(a)The shares reported in this column include 101,602 shares related to our purchases of shares from participants in our stock-based compensation plans in connection with the vesting of restricted stock and other stock compensation transactions in accordance with the terms of our stock-based compensation plans.

(b)The average price paid per share reported in this column excludes brokerage commissions and a one percent excise tax on share purchases.

(c)On February 22, 2024, we announced that our Board authorized us to purchase shares of our outstanding common stock for a total cost of up to $2.5 billion with no expiration date (the February 2024 Program). As of December 31, 2024, we had $1.8 billion remaining available for purchase under the February 2024 Program. On October 29, 2024, we announced that our Board authorized us to purchase shares of our outstanding common stock for a total cost of up to $2.5 billion with no expiration date (the September 2024 Program), which is in addition to the amount remaining under the February 2024 Program. This authorization was granted on September 19, 2024.

Our selected peer group comprises the following eleven members: ConocoPhillips; CVR Energy, Inc.; Delek US Holdings, Inc.; the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund; EOG Resources, Inc.; HF Sinclair Corporation; LyondellBasell Industries N.V.; Marathon Petroleum Corporation; Occidental Petroleum Corporation; PBF Energy Inc.; and Phillips 66. The Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLE) serves as a proxy for stock price performance of the energy sector and includes energy companies with which we compete for capital. We believe that our peer group represents a group of companies for making head-to-head performance comparisons in a competitive operating environment that is primarily characterized by U.S.-based companies that have business models predominantly consisting of downstream refining operations, together with similarly sized energy companies that share operating similarities to us, and that are in adjacent segments of the oil and gas industry.

tasks.description: What was the return on plan assets (ROPA) for VALERO ENERGY CORP/TX's pension plans in 2025, and how did it compare to the previous year?
tasks.gold_answer:
According to the 10-K filing, the actual return on plan assets for VALERO ENERGY CORP/TX's pension plans in 2025 was $310 million. To calculate the ROPA, we can divide the actual return by the fair value of plan assets at the beginning of the year.

ROPA (2025) = Actual Return / Fair Value of Plan Assets (beginning of year)
= $310 million / $2,835 million
= 10.94%

In 2024, the actual return on plan assets was $399 million, and the fair value of plan assets at the beginning of the year was $2,485 million.

ROPA (2024) = Actual Return / Fair Value of Plan Assets (beginning of year)
= $399 million / $2,485 million
= 16.06%

Comparing the two years, we can see that the ROPA decreased by 5.12 percentage points from 2024 to 2025, indicating a decline in the investment performance of VALERO ENERGY CORP/TX's pension plans. This could have implications for the company's future pension obligations and funding requirements.
key_points: The actual return on plan assets for 2025 must be stated as $310 million.
The calculated ROPA percentage for 2025 must be provided (10.94%).
The actual return on plan assets for 2024 must be stated as $399 million.
The calculated ROPA percentage for 2024 must be provided (16.06%).
A comparison between the 2025 and 2024 ROPA must be made, noting the decrease of 5.12 percentage points.

tasks.gold_answer:
According to the 10-K filing, VALERO ENERGY CORP/TX has an enterprise approach to information security risk management and governance. The company's information security program and framework comprise processes, policies, practices, systems, and technologies designed to identify, assess, prioritize, manage, and monitor risks to its information systems. This includes risks from cybersecurity threats and events, as well as risks associated with the use of third-party service providers.

In 2025, the company continues to assess the risks and opportunities from conventional and generative AI, which was initiated in 2024. The company's established recovery approach is designed to provide for the ready availability and use of its business-critical processes in the event of any downtime, disaster, or outages. Additionally, the control environment and internal audit process are designed to bring a systematic, disciplined approach to evaluate risk management, control, and governance processes concerning cybersecurity and the information security framework.

The company's cybersecurity Incident Response Plan (IRP) sets forth a process designed to effectively respond to an incident by obtaining information, coordinating activities, assessing results, and communicating applicable developments to stakeholders. The IRP includes major components such as preparation, detection and analysis, containment, eradication, notification, recovery, reporting, and lessons learned.

Overall, VALERO ENERGY CORP/TX's information security program and framework contribute significantly to the company's overall risk management strategy by identifying, assessing, and mitigating cybersecurity risks, ensuring business continuity, and protecting sensitive data.

The answer must explain how the information security program contributes to the company's overall risk management strategy
The answer must mention specific components of the information security framework (e.g., processes, policies, practices, systems, technologies)

The answer must include specific elements mentioned in the 10-K filing such as the Incident Response Plan (IRP) and business continuity/recovery approach

results.final_answer:
ITEM 1B. UNRESOLVED STAFF COMMENTS

None.

ITEM 1C. CYBERSECURITY

RISK MANAGEMENT AND STRATEGY

We take an enterprise approach to information security risk management and governance. Our information security program and framework comprise processes, policies, practices, systems, and technologies that are designed to identify, assess, prioritize, manage, and monitor risks to our information systems, including risks from cybersecurity threats and events and risks associated with the use of third-party service providers.

Our established recovery approach is designed to provide for the ready availability and use of our business-critical processes in the event of any downtime, disaster, or outages. We also seek to identify and mitigate the risks associated with the use of third-party service providers through the review of their security programs prior to our engagement thereof. Additionally, our control environment and internal audit process are designed to bring a systematic, disciplined approach to evaluate our risk management, control, and governance processes concerning cybersecurity and our information security framework.

We have a cybersecurity Incident Response Plan (IRP) that sets forth a process designed to effectively respond to an incident by obtaining information, coordinating activities, assessing results, and communicating applicable developments to our stakeholders, including employees, law enforcement, other external parties and agencies, and our Board. The IRP includes the following major components: preparation, detection and analysis, containment, eradication, notification, recovery, reporting, and lessons learned. Specific technical and legal playbooks have also been developed for data breaches, malware, unauthorized remote access, and ransomware. We have also retained certain third-party experts to assist us with various aspects of incident assessment and response in the event those services become necessary or useful.

Typically, we (i) perform periodic tabletop exercises with a company-wide cross-functional team that are facilitated by a third-party expert and are intended to simulate a real-life security incident, (ii) conduct penetration testing as needed and annually conduct Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard testing and firewall reviews, and have periodically engaged a third-party expert to help therewith, (iii) hold annual cybersecurity awareness trainings, and (iv) periodically engage a third-party expert to conduct a review of our information security framework, which is designed to help identify existing and emerging risks, and mitigate against such risks. These internal efforts and external third-party reviews also support our efforts to regularly assess our information security program and framework against emerging risks, market and industry developments and provide opportunities to make adjustments or enhancements when deemed prudent or necessary. In 2024, we established a company-wide cross-functional team to preliminarily assess the risks and opportunities from conventional and generative AI and will continue these assessments in 2025. To date, there have been no cybersecurity incidents that have materially affected us, or that are reasonably likely to materially affect us, including our business strategy, financial condition, or results of operations.

For additional information on the cybersecurity risks we face, see “ITEM 1A. RISK FACTORS—Cybersecurity and Privacy Related Risks—We are subject to risks arising from a significant breach of our information systems.”

32

GOVERNANCE

Our Board’s Role in Cybersecurity Oversight

Oversight of risk management, including with respect to risks from cybersecurity threats, is the responsibility of our Board, which exercises its oversight responsibilities both directly and through its committees. The Audit Committee of our Board has formal oversight responsibilities established in its committee charter concerning our initiatives and strategies respecting cybersecurity and information technology risks. At least once annually, the heads of our information services and internal audit teams provide a report to the Audit Committee on cybersecurity and information technology risks, as well as our information security operations, structure, framework, various cybersecurity and information technology metrics, our cybersecurity and information security management and improvement efforts, future projects, and our governance and assessments related to cybersecurity and information technology. The chair of the Audit Committee reports to the Board a summary of the information presented by the heads of our information services and internal audit teams during their cybersecurity update. Periodically, the Board also receives reports on such matters directly. As noted above, the IRP also contains notification procedures to the Board.

Management’s Role in Assessment and Management of Material Risks from Cybersecurity Threats

We have an Information Security Committee (Infosec Committee) consisting of refining, renewable diesel, ethanol, logistics, and information services personnel that meets weekly to evaluate third-party exchange of data and collaborate on strategy for dealing with information security risks and other related matters. The Infosec Committee reports to our Information Security Oversight Committee (Infosec Oversight Committee) and our Executive Steering Committee on cybersecurity (Executive Steering Committee). Our Infosec Oversight Committee consists of information services, refining, and internal audit personnel and meets quarterly to discuss network threats and the overall security landscape. Ou

tasks.description: What was the average age of the executive officers of VALERO ENERGY CORP/TX as of December 31, 2024, and what insights can be drawn from this metric regarding leadership tenure and potential succession planning in 2025?
tasks.gold_answer:
According to the provided information, the ages of the executive officers of VALERO ENERGY CORP/TX as of December 31, 2024, are:

* R. Lane Riggs: 59
* Jason W. Fraser: 56
* Gary K. Simmons: 60
* Richard J. Walsh: 59
* Eric A. Fisher: 56

The average age of the executive officers is approximately 58 years old.

This metric suggests that VALERO ENERGY CORP/TX has a relatively experienced and mature leadership team, with most executives in their late 50s or early 60s. This could indicate a level of stability and continuity in the company's leadership, which can be beneficial for strategic planning and execution.

However, it also raises questions about potential succession planning and leadership tenure. With several executives nearing traditional retirement age, VALERO ENERGY CORP/TX may need to consider grooming younger leaders or identifying external candidates to ensure a smooth transition of power in the future. This could be a key area of focus for the company in 2025 and beyond.

results.final_answer:
ITEM 4. MINE SAFETY DISCLOSURES

None.

INFORMATION ABOUT OUR EXECUTIVE OFFICERS

The following table lists the names and titles of our executive officers (for purposes of Rule 3b-7 under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934) as of the date of this report. There is no arrangement or understanding between any executive officer listed below or any other person under which the executive officer was or is to be selected as an officer. Under our bylaws, our officers are elected annually by our Board, and hold such office until their successor has been chosen and qualified, or their earlier death, resignation, or removal.

|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  | | |  | | |  |  |  |
| Name | | |  | | | Current Position | | |  | | |  | | |  | | | Age as of  December 31,  2024 | | |
| R. Lane Riggs | | |  | | | Chairman of the Board, Chief Executive Officer and President | | |  | | |  | | |  | | | 59 | | |
| Jason W. Fraser | | |  | | | Executive Vice President and Chief Financial Officer | | |  | | |  | | |  | | | 56 | | |
| Gary K. Simmons | | |  | | | Executive Vice President and Chief Operating Officer | | |  | | |  | | |  | | | 60 | | |
| Richard J. Walsh | | |  | | | Executive Vice President and General Counsel | | |  | | |  | | |  | | | 59 | | |
| Eric A. Fisher | | |  | | | Senior Vice President Product Supply, Trading and Wholesale | | |  | | |  | | |  | | | 56 | | |

Mr. Riggs was elected to the additional position of Chairman of the Board as of the close of business on December 31, 2024, and was elected Chief Executive Officer and President, and as a member of our Board effective as of the close of business on June 30, 2023. He previously served as President and Chief Operating Officer (beginning January 23, 2020), and Executive Vice President and Chief Operating Officer (beginning January 1, 2018), and prior to that as Executive Vice President Refining Operations and Engineering (beginning 2014), and Senior Vice President Refining Operations (beginning 2011). He has held several leadership positions with Valero overseeing refining operations, supply optimization and crude and feedstock supply, and planning and economics. Mr. Riggs also previously served on the board of directors of Valero Energy Partners GP LLC (the general partner of Valero Energy Partners LP) from 2014 to 2019.

Mr. Fraser was elected Executive Vice President and Chief Financial Officer effective July 15, 2020. Prior to that he served as Executive Vice President and General Counsel (beginning January 1, 2019). In 2018, he served as Senior Vice President overseeing Valero’s Public Policy & Strategic Planning, Governmental Affairs, Investor Relations, and External Communications functions. From November 2016 to May 2018, Mr. Fraser served as Vice President Public Policy & Strategic Planning, and from May 2015 to November 2016, he served in London as Vice President Europe, overseeing our European commercial businesses. Prior to his service in London, he held various leadership positions at our San Antonio headquarters, including Senior Vice President & Deputy General Counsel and Senior Vice President Specialty Products in the Valero family of companies.

Mr. Simmons was elected Executive Vice President and Chief Operating Officer on July 20, 2023. He previously served as Executive Vice President and Chief Commercial Officer (beginning January 23, 2020), and Senior Vice President Supply, International Operations and Systems Optimization (beginning May 2014), and prior to that as Vice President Crude and Feedstock Supply and Trading (2012 to 2014), and Vice President Supply Chain Optimization (2011 to 2012). Mr. Simmons has held many leadership positions with Valero, including Vice President and General Manager of our Ardmore and St. Charles refineries.

Mr. Walsh was elected Executive Vice President and General Counsel on October 29, 2024. He previously served as Senior Vice President, General Counsel and Secretary, (beginning April 22, 2021), and prior to that he served as Senior Vice President and General Counsel (beginning July 15, 2020). Mr. Walsh has responsibility for our legal and governmental affairs, health, safety, and environmental, fuels compliance, risk management, ESG, and compliance/ethics teams. He previously served as Vice President and Deputy General Counsel from 2016 to 2020. He joined Valero in 1999 and has served in many different leadership roles within our legal department.

Mr. Fisher was elected Senior Vice President Product Supply, Trading and Wholesale on July 20, 2023. He previously served as Senior Vice President Wholesale Marketing & International Commercial Operations from 2017 to 2023. In his current role, Mr. Fisher has responsibility for our product supply and trading, global wholesale marketing, and the specialty products marketing business. He joined Valero in 1997 and has held many leadership positions within the Company, including President-Europe, Vice President-Investor and Corporate Communications, and Vice President-Investor Relations, and Marketing and Strategic Planning.

In 2018, he served as Senior Vice President overseeing Valero’s Public Policy & Strategic Planning, Governmental Affairs, Investor Relations, and External Communications functions. From November 2016 to May 2018, Mr. Fraser served as Vice President Public Policy & Strategic Planning, and from May 2015 to November 2016, he served in London as Vice President Europe, overseeing our European commercial businesses. Prior to his service in London, he held various leadership positions at our San Antonio headquarters, including Senior Vice President & Deputy General Counsel and Senior Vice President Specialty Products in the Valero family of companies.

tasks.description: What was the trend in Valero Energy Corp/TX's environmental enforcement matters settlements during 2020, and what were the associated civil penalties?
tasks.gold_answer: During 2020, Valero Energy Corp/TX settled several environmental enforcement matters with various authorities. The trend suggests that the company was able to resolve these matters without incurring material financial impacts. Specifically, on January 27, 2020, the Texas AG filed a lawsuit related to excess emissions from Tank 003 at the Houston Terminal, which was resolved on September 30, 2020, for civil penalties in an amount not material to Valero. Similarly, on October 19, 2020, the company resolved a matter with the U.S. EPA for injunctive relief and civil penalties also deemed not material. Notably, the TCEQ withdrew its Notice of Enforcement related to the Corpus Christi West Refinery on September 25, 2020, without proceeding with formal enforcement. The only outstanding matter is the NOE from the TCEQ related to a RCRA inspection at the Three Rivers Refinery, which could result in penalties exceeding $100,000; however, the company is working to resolve this matter.
key_points: The overall trend in Valero Energy Corp/TX's environmental enforcement matters settlements during 2020
Specific details about the environmental enforcement matters that were settled in 2020
The associated civil penalties for these settlements (including whether they were material or not)
Mention of any outstanding/unresolved enforcement matters from 2020
Identification of the regulatory authorities involved in the enforcement matters

results.final_answer:
ITEM 1. LEGAL PROCEEDINGS

The information below describes new proceedings or material developments in proceedings that we previously reported in our annual report on Form 10-K for the year ended December 31, 2019 and our quarterly reports on Form 10-Q for the quarterly periods ended March 31, 2020, and June 30, 2020.

Environmental Enforcement Matters

While it is not possible to predict the outcome of the following environmental proceedings, if any one or more of them were decided against us, we believe that there would be no material effect on our financial position, results of operations, or liquidity. We are reporting these proceedings to comply with SEC regulations, which require us to disclose certain information about proceedings arising under federal, state, or local provisions regulating the discharge of materials into the environment or protecting the environment if we reasonably believe that such proceedings will result in monetary sanctions of $100,000 or more.

Attorney General of the State of Texas (Texas AG) (Houston Terminal). In our annual report on Form 10-K for the year ended December 31, 2019, we reported that we had an outstanding Notice of Enforcement (NOE) from the Texas Commission on Environmental Quality (TCEQ), and an outstanding Violation Notice from the Harris County Pollution Control Services Department, both alleging excess emissions from Tank 003 that occurred during Hurricane Harvey at our Houston Terminal. On January 27, 2020, the Texas AG filed suit related to this incident against our Houston Terminal in the 419th Judicial District Court of Travis County, Texas, Cause No. D-1-GN-20-000516. On September 30, 2020, we resolved this matter with the Texas AG for civil penalties in an amount not material to Valero.

U.S. EPA (Fuels). In our annual report on Form 10-K for the year ended December 31, 2019, we reported that we had an outstanding Notice of Violation and draft Consent Order from the U.S. EPA related to violations from a 2015 Mobile Source Inspection. On October 19, 2020, we resolved this matter with the EPA for injunctive relief and civil penalties in an amount not material to Valero.

TCEQ (Corpus Christi West Refinery). In our Quarterly Report on Form 10-Q for the quarter ended June 30, 2020, we reported that we had received an NOE from the TCEQ related to a Title V deviation report investigation at our Corpus Christ West Refinery. On September 25, 2020, the TCEQ withdrew this NOE and is not proceeding with formal enforcement.

TCEQ (Three Rivers Refinery). On August 31, 2020, we received an NOE from the TCEQ related to a Resource Conservation and Recovery Act (RCRA) inspection at our Three Rivers Refinery, which we believe could result in penalties in excess of $100,000. We are working with the TCEQ to resolve this matter.

tasks.description: What was the total value of shares purchased by VALERO ENERGY CORP/TX during the third quarter of 2020, and how does this impact their liquidity position?
tasks.gold_answer:
According to the filing, VALERO ENERGY CORP/TX purchased a total of 5,094 shares in the third quarter of 2020, with an average price paid per share of $52.56. The total value of these purchases can be calculated as:

Total Value = Total Number of Shares Purchased x Average Price Paid per Share
= 5,094 shares x $52.56 per share
= $267,740.64

This represents a significant use of cash resources by the company, which could impact their liquidity position. However, it's worth noting that the company still has $1.4 billion remaining available for purchase under its 2018 share repurchase program, indicating that they have sufficient liquidity to continue making these purchases if desired. The decision to pause share repurchases since mid-March 2020 may also be a strategic move to conserve cash and maintain flexibility in response to changing market conditions.
key_points: The total value of shares purchased must be calculated and stated as $267,740.64
The calculation must be based on the correct figures: 5,094 shares and an average price of $52.56 per share
The answer must explicitly state the impact of this purchase on the company's liquidity position
The answer must mention the remaining $1.4 billion available under the share repurchase program as context for liquidity
The answer must acknowledge the pause in share repurchases since mid-March 2020 as relevant to the liquidity strategy

tasks.description: What is the potential impact on VALERO ENERGY CORP/TX's fair value of derivative instruments due to a 10% increase or decrease in underlying commodity prices as of June 30, 2022?
tasks.gold_answer: According to the sensitivity analysis as of June 30, 2022, the impact of changes in underlying commodity prices on the fair value of VALERO ENERGY CORP/TX's derivatives is as follows:/nIf the underlying commodity price increases by 10%, it would result in a fair value loss of $60 million./nIf the underlying commodity price decreases by 10%, it would result in a fair value gain of $60 million./nThis inverse relationship indicates that, at that time, the company's derivative portfolio (such as short positions or hedging instruments used to lock in purchase costs) allowed it to benefit from a decline in commodity prices and face losses when prices rose, reflecting the characteristics of the hedging strategy employed to manage price volatility risks associated with its inventory and purchase commitments.
key_points: The specific monetary impact for a 10% price increase: a fair value loss of $60 million.
The specific monetary impact for a 10% price decrease: a fair value gain of $60 million.
The date for which the sensitivity analysis is valid: as of June 30, 2022.
The direction of the relationship: an inverse relationship where price increases cause losses and price decreases cause gains.
The subject of the impact: the fair value of Valero Energy Corp/TX's derivative instruments.

results.final_answer:
ITEM 3. QUANTITATIVE AND QUALITATIVE DISCLOSURES ABOUT MARKET RISK

COMMODITY PRICE RISK

We are exposed to market risks related to the volatility in the price of feedstocks (primarily crude oil, waste and renewable feedstocks, and corn), the products we produce, and natural gas used in our operations. To reduce the impact of price volatility on our results of operations and cash flows, we use commodity derivative instruments, including futures and options to manage the volatility of:

•inventories and firm commitments to purchase inventories generally for amounts by which our current year inventory levels (determined on a LIFO basis) differ from our previous year-end LIFO inventory levels; and

•forecasted purchases and/or product sales at existing market prices that we deem favorable.

Our positions in commodity derivative instruments are monitored and managed on a daily basis by our risk control group to ensure compliance with our stated risk management policy that has been approved by our Board.

The following sensitivity analysis includes all of our derivative instruments entered into for purposes other than trading with which we have market risk (in millions):

|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  | | | June 30, 2022 | | |  | | | December 31, 2021 | | |
| Gain (loss) in fair value resulting from: | | |  | | |  | | |  | | |
| 10% increase in underlying commodity prices | | | $ | (60) |  |  | | | $ | (61) |  |
| 10% decrease in underlying commodity prices | | | 60 | |  |  | | | 61 | |  |

See Note 14 of Condensed Notes to Consolidated Financial Statements for notional volumes associated with these derivative contracts as of June 30, 2022.

COMPLIANCE PROGRAM PRICE RISK

We are exposed to market risk related to the volatility in the price of credits needed to comply with the Renewable and Low-Carbon Fuel Blending Programs. To manage this risk, we enter into contracts to purchase these credits. As of June 30, 2022 and December 31, 2021, the amount of gain or loss in the fair value of derivative instruments that would have resulted from a 10 percent increase or decrease in the underlying price of the contracts was not material. See Note 14 of Condensed Notes to Consolidated Financial Statements for a discussion about these blending programs.

results.final_answer:
ITEM 3. QUANTITATIVE AND QUALITATIVE DISCLOSURES ABOUT MARKET RISK

COMMODITY PRICE RISK

We are exposed to market risks related to the volatility in the price of feedstocks (primarily crude oil, waste and renewable feedstocks, and corn), the products we produce, and natural gas used in our operations. To reduce the impact of price volatility on our results of operations and cash flows, we use commodity derivative instruments, including futures and options to manage the volatility of:

•inventories and firm commitments to purchase inventories generally for amounts by which our current year inventory levels (determined on a LIFO basis) differ from our previous year-end LIFO inventory levels; and

•forecasted purchases and/or product sales at existing market prices that we deem favorable.

Our positions in commodity derivative instruments are monitored and managed on a daily basis by our risk control group to ensure compliance with our stated risk management policy that has been approved by our Board.

The following sensitivity analysis includes all of our derivative instruments entered into for purposes other than trading with which we have market risk (in millions):

| 10% increase in underlying commodity prices | | | $ | (60) |  |  | | | $ | (61) |  |
| 10% decrease in underlying commodity prices | | | 60 | |  |  | | | 61 | |  |

See Note 14 of Condensed Notes to Consolidated Financial Statements for notional volumes associated with these derivative contracts as of June 30, 2022.

COMPLIANCE PROGRAM PRICE RISK

We are exposed to market risk related to the volatility in the price of credits needed to comply with the Renewable and Low-Carbon Fuel Blending Programs. To manage this risk, we enter into contracts to purchase these credits. As of June 30, 2022 and December 31, 2021, the amount of gain or loss in the fair value of derivative instruments that would have resulted from a 10 percent increase or decrease in the underlying price of the contracts was not material. See Note 14 of Condensed Notes to Consolidated Financial Statements for a discussion about these blending programs.

tasks.description: What was the total amount spent by VALERO ENERGY CORP/TX on share repurchases during the second quarter of 2022, and how does this compare to their remaining authorization as of June 30, 2022?
tasks.gold_answer: Based on the table data, the total amount spent on share repurchases in Q2 2022 was approximately $1.75 billion (14,211,408 shares × $123.01 per share). As of June 30, 2022, the remaining authorization under the then-existing plan was $0. All authorized purchases under that program were completed during the quarter ended June 30, 2022. The $863 million mentioned in the answer was the balance after May 2022, not the quarter-end balance. On July 7, 2022, the company's Board did authorize an additional $2.5 billion for share repurchases with no expiration date. Although the old program was exhausted at quarter-end, the immediate initiation of a larger new program demonstrates the company's ongoing commitment to returning capital to shareholders via buybacks.
key_points: The total amount spent on share repurchases in Q2 2022 was approximately $1.75 billion.
The remaining authorization under the program in effect during Q2 2022 was $0 as of June 30, 2022.
The $0 remaining authorization is because the program was fully exhausted/completed during the quarter ended June 30, 2022.

tasks.description: What was the strategic significance of Ms. Ffolkes's election to the Board of Directors for VALERO ENERGY CORP/TX in 2022, and how might this impact the company's future performance?
tasks.gold_answer: The election of Ms. Ffolkes to the Board of Directors is a strategically significant event for VALERO ENERGY CORP/TX in 2022, as it indicates the company's efforts to strengthen its leadership and governance structure. This move may have long-term implications on the company's decision-making processes, risk management, and overall performance. As Ms. Ffolkes is expected to stand for re-election at the 2023 Annual Meeting, this development suggests a level of continuity and stability in the company's leadership, which can be positively received by investors and stakeholders. Furthermore, this event may also signal VALERO ENERGY CORP/TX's commitment to diversity and inclusion, as Ms. Ffolkes brings her expertise and perspectives to the Board. Overall, this strategic move could contribute to a more robust and effective governance structure, ultimately enhancing the company's ability to navigate complex energy markets and drive long-term growth.

tasks.description: What are the strategic implications of Joseph W. Gorder's retirement as Executive Chairman and board member on VALERO ENERGY CORP/TX's leadership structure in 2024?
tasks.gold_answer: The retirement of Joseph W. Gorder as Executive Chairman and board member, effective December 31, 2024, will result in a decrease in the size of the Board to 10 directors. This change may have significant strategic implications for VALERO ENERGY CORP/TX's leadership structure and governance. The election of R. Lane Riggs, the current CEO and President, as Chairman of the Board may indicate a smooth transition of power and maintain stability within the organization. However, this change may also lead to potential shifts in decision-making processes, risk management strategies, and overall company direction. As VALERO ENERGY CORP/TX navigates this leadership transition, investors should closely monitor the company's performance and assess how effectively the new leadership structure addresses emerging challenges and opportunities in the energy sector.
key_points: The retirement of Joseph W. Gorder as Executive Chairman and board member is effective December 31, 2024.
The retirement results in a decrease in the size of the Board of Directors to 10 members.
R. Lane Riggs, the current CEO and President, is elected as the new Chairman of the Board.
The leadership change implies a transition of power and aims to maintain stability.
The change may lead to shifts in decision-making processes, risk management, and company direction.

results.final_answer:
| Item 5.02 | Departure of Directors or Certain Officers; Election of Directors; Appointment of Certain Officers; Compensatory Arrangements of Certain Officers. |

On October 30, 2024, Valero Energy Corporation (“Valero” or the “Company”) announced that Joseph W. Gorder has elected to retire from his position as Executive Chairman and as a member of the of the board of directors of Valero (the “Board”) effective as of the close of business on December 31, 2024 (the “Effective Date”). Valero also announced that the Board has elected R. Lane Riggs, Valero’s Chief Executive Officer and President, and member of the Board, to the additional position of Chairman of the Board effective as of the Effective Date.

With the retirement of Mr. Gorder from the Board, the size of the Board will be decreased to 10 directors effective as of the Effective Date.

Safe Harbor Statement

tasks.description: In 2020, what were the key governance policies and internal control mechanisms reported by CADIZ_INC and HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP, and how do these compare in terms of effectiveness and their influence on company performance?
tasks.gold_answer: In 2020, CADIZ_INC and HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP demonstrated distinct approaches to governance policies and internal controls, as per their Item 10 disclosures. CADIZ_INC explicitly reported that its disclosure controls and internal control over financial reporting were effective as of December 31, 2020, indicating a strong governance framework that ensures accurate financial reporting and compliance. This effectiveness is a strength that could enhance investor confidence and support strategic decisions by minimizing errors and fraud risks, potentially improving performance through reliable data for resource allocation. In contrast, HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP's filing noted that its corporate governance structure would be detailed in a later proxy statement, suggesting incomplete disclosure at the time, with no specific mention of internal control effectiveness. The context highlights Houston American's experienced leadership as a strength, but the lack of explicit governance policies in Item 10 implies a potential gap in transparency, which might raise concerns about oversight and risk management. Comparatively, Cadiz's affirmed control effectiveness aligns with its smaller reporting company status, possibly enabling more agile governance, while Houston American's deferred disclosure could indicate a less immediate focus on governance rigor, potentially impacting performance if unaddressed risks emerge. For instance, Cadiz's controls may have helped manage its financial position (with assets of $74.36 million and liabilities of $99.66 million), whereas Houston American's reliance on experienced leadership alone might suffice for operational strategy but lack the structured safeguards seen in Cadiz. Ultimately, Cadiz's governance policies appear more verifiably robust in 2020, potentially leading to better risk mitigation and long-term stability, while Houston American's approach hinges on leadership expertise, which could drive performance in the short term but require bolstering with formal controls for sustained governance.
key_points: The answer must explicitly state the key governance policies and internal control mechanisms reported by CADIZ_INC for 2020, specifically mentioning the effectiveness of its disclosure controls and internal control over financial reporting as of December 31, 2020.

The answer must explicitly state the key governance policies and internal control mechanisms reported by HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP for 2020, specifically noting the deferred disclosure of corporate governance structure and the lack of a specific mention of internal control effectiveness in its Item 10 filing.

The answer must provide a direct comparison between the two companies' reported governance and control mechanisms in terms of their effectiveness, highlighting Cadiz's affirmed effectiveness versus Houston American's lack of explicit affirmation and incomplete disclosure.

The answer must discuss how these differing governance and control approaches influence or could influence company performance, linking Cadiz's effective controls to potential benefits like investor confidence and risk mitigation, and Houston American's approach to potential transparency gaps or reliance on leadership expertise.

**Report of Independent Registered Public Accounting Firm**

Tothe Board of Directors and Stockholders of Cadiz Inc.

***Opinion on the Financial Statements***

We have audited the accompanying consolidated balance sheets of Cadiz Inc. and its subsidiaries (the “Company”) as of December 31, 2020 and 2019, and the related consolidated statements of operations and comprehensive loss, of stockholders’ deficit and of cash flows for the years then ended, including the related notes (collectively referred to as the “consolidated financial statements”). In our opinion, the consolidated financial statements present fairly, in all material respects, the financial position of the Company as of December 31, 2020 and 2019, and the results of its operations and its cash flows for the years then ended in conformity with accounting principles generally accepted in the United States of America.

***Basis for Opinion***

These consolidated financial statements are the responsibility of the Company’s management. Our responsibility is to express an opinion on the Company’s consolidated financial statements based on our audits. We are a public accounting firm registered with the Public Company Accounting Oversight Board (United States) (PCAOB) and are required to be independent with respect to the Company in accordance with the U.S. federal securities laws and the applicable rules and regulations of the Securities and Exchange Commission and the PCAOB.

We conducted our audits of these consolidated financial statements in accordance with the standards of the PCAOB. Those standards require that we plan and perform the audit to obtain reasonable assurance about whether the consolidated financial statements are free of material misstatement, whether due to error or fraud. The Company is not required to have, nor were we engaged to perform, an audit of its internal control over financial reporting. As part of our audits we are required to obtain an understanding of internal control over financial reporting but not for the purpose of expressing an opinion on the effectiveness of the Company's internal control over financial reporting. Accordingly, we express no such opinion.

Our audits included performing procedures to assess the risks of material misstatement of the consolidated financial statements, whether due to error or fraud, and performing procedures that respond to those risks. Such procedures included examining, on a test basis, evidence regarding the amounts and disclosures in the consolidated financial statements. Our audits also included evaluating the accounting principles used and significant estimates made by management, as well as evaluating the overall presentation of the consolidated financial statements. We believe that our audits provide a reasonable basis for our opinion.

***Critical Audit Matters***

The critical audit matter communicated below is a matter arising from the current period audit of the consolidated financial statements that was communicated or required to be communicated to the audit committee and that (i) relates to accounts or disclosures that are material to the consolidated financial statements and (ii) involved our especially challenging, subjective, or complex judgments. The communication of critical audit matters does not alter in any way our opinion on the consolidated financial statem

tasks.description: How did the corporate governance practices of CADIZ_INC and HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP in 2020 address potential risks related to executive roles and board oversight, and what implications might this have for their strategic direction and financial health?
tasks.gold_answer: In 2020, the corporate governance practices of CADIZ_INC and HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP addressed risks related to executive roles and board oversight in differing ways, based on the context information. For CADIZ_INC, as a smaller reporting company, the effective internal controls over financial reporting suggest a proactive approach to mitigating risks associated with executive decision-making and financial misstatements. This implies that board oversight likely included rigorous monitoring mechanisms, which could reduce the risk of fraud or errors impacting strategic direction, such as ensuring that resource allocations align with company goals despite Cadiz's liability-heavy balance sheet (total liabilities of $99.66 million versus assets of $74.36 million). In contrast, HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP's governance highlighted that executive officers, including CEO John Terwilliger, serve at the discretion of the Board, with no family relationships among executives and directors, which minimizes risks of nepotism and enhances independence in oversight. However, the deferred disclosure of full governance structure in a proxy statement indicates that comprehensive board oversight details were not immediately available, potentially leaving gaps in risk assessment for strategic decisions. The experienced leadership of Houston American (with Terwilliger's 40+ years in oil and gas) may offset this by providing seasoned guidance, reducing operational risks in a volatile industry. Comparatively, Cadiz's governance practices appear more formally structured with verified controls, likely supporting a strategic direction focused on stability and compliance, whereas Houston American's reliance on board discretion and leadership expertise might favor agile, experience-driven strategies. These differences could impact financial health: Cadiz's controls may help navigate its negative net worth by ensuring prudent management, while Houston American's approach could lead to innovative ventures but with higher uncertainty if oversight is not fully documented. Overall, Cadiz's governance seems better equipped to handle financial and operational risks in 2020, potentially leading to more predictable performance, while Houston American's practices emphasize leadership strength, which may drive growth but require enhanced transparency for long-term resilience.
key_points: The answer must compare the corporate governance practices of CADIZ_INC and HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP in 2020 regarding executive roles and board oversight.
For CADIZ_INC, the answer must mention its status as a smaller reporting company and the effectiveness of its internal controls over financial reporting as a key governance mechanism addressing risk.
For HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP, the answer must mention the Board's discretion over executive officers and the absence of family relationships among executives/directors as governance features.
The answer must discuss the implications of these differing governance practices for each company's strategic direction.
The answer must discuss the implications of these differing governance practices for each company's financial health.

***Opinion on the Financial Statements***

We have audited the accompanying consolidated balance sheets of Cadiz Inc. and its subsidiaries (the “Company”) as of December 31, 2020 and 2019, and the related consolidated statements of operations and comprehensive loss, of stockholders’ deficit and of cash flows for the years then ended, including the related notes (collectively referred to as the “consolidated financial statements”). In our opinion, the consolidated financial statements present fairly, in all material respects, the financial position of the Company as of December 31, 2020 and 2019, and the results of its operations and its cash flows for the years then ended in conformity with accounting principles generally accepted in the United States of America.

***Basis for Opinion***

These consolidated financial statements are the responsibility of the Company’s management. Our responsibility is to express an opinion on the Company’s consolidated financial statements based on our audits. We are a public accounting firm registered with the Public Company Accounting Oversight Board (United States) (PCAOB) and are required to be independent with respect to the Company in accordance with the U.S. federal securities laws and the applicable rules and regulations of the Securities and Exchange Commission and the PCAOB.

We conducted our audits of these consolidated financial statements in accordance with the standards of the PCAOB. Those standards require that we plan and perform the audit to obtain reasonable assurance about whether the consolidated financial statements are free of material misstatement, whether due to error or fraud. The Company is not required to have, nor were we engaged to perform, an audit of its internal control over financial reporting. As part of our audits we are required to obtain an understanding of internal control over financial reporting but not for the purpose of expressing an opinion on the effectiveness of the Company's internal control over financial reporting. Accordingly, we express no such opinion.

Our audits included performing procedures to assess the risks of material misstatement of the consolidated financial statements, whether due to error or fraud, and performing procedures that respond to those risks. Such procedures included examining, on a test basis, evidence regarding the amounts and disclosures in the consolidated financial statements. Our audits also included evaluating the accounting principles used and significant estimates made by management, as well as evaluating the overall presentation of the consolidated financial statements. We believe that our audits provide a reasonable basis for our opinion.

***Critical Audit Matters***

The critical audit matter communicated below is a matter arising from the current period audit of the consolidated financial statements that was communicated or required to be communicated to the audit committee and that (i) relates to accounts or disclosures that are material to the consolidated financial statements and (ii) involved our especially challenging, subjective, or complex judgments. tasks.description: How did the corporate governance structure and leadership experience at CADIZ_INC and HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP in 2020 relate to their respective financial performance, particularly in terms of asset management and liabilities?
tasks.gold_answer: In 2020, CADIZ_INC and HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP exhibited distinct governance structures and leadership experiences that correlated with their financial outcomes. CADIZ_INC, as a smaller reporting company, maintained effective internal controls over financial reporting, which supported stable asset management; total assets were $74.36 million, with other assets decreasing by 13% due to reduced prepaid rent, and accrued liabilities dropping significantly by 85%, reflecting disciplined cost control. This governance strength likely contributed to consistent stock-based compensation and managed fluctuations in liabilities. In contrast, HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP had experienced leadership under CEO John Terwilliger, with over 40 years in oil and gas, but its governance structure was incompletely disclosed, pending a proxy statement. Financially, this company saw a 45% decline in revenue to $552,345 and a widened net loss to $4.04 million, though total liabilities decreased by 67% and cash increased substantially due to equity issuances of $4.43 million. The comparison suggests that CADIZ_INC's robust internal controls may have fostered better operational stability, while HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP's leadership experience did not prevent revenue declines, though it facilitated strategic financing to improve liquidity.

We have audited the accompanying consolidated balance sheets of Cadiz Inc. and its subsidiaries (the “Company”) as of December 31, 2020 and 2019, and the related consolidated statements of operations and comprehensive loss, of stockholders’ deficit and of cash flows for the years then ended, including the related notes (collectively referred to as the “consolidated financial statements”). In our opinion, the consolidated financial statements present fairly, in all material respects, the financial position of the Company as of December 31, 2020 and 2019, and the results of its operations and its cash flows for the years then ended in conformity with accounting principles generally accepted in the United States of America.

***Basis for Opinion***

These consolidated financial statements are the responsibility of the Company’s management. Our responsibility is to express an opinion on the Company’s consolidated financial statements based on our audits. We are a public accounting firm registered with the Public Company Accounting Oversight Board (United States) (PCAOB) and are required to be independent with respect to the Company in accordance with the U.S. federal securities laws and the applicable rules and regulations of the Securities and Exchange Commission and the PCAOB.

We conducted our audits of these consolidated financial statements in accordance with the standards of the PCAOB. Those standards require that we plan and perform the audit to obtain reasonable assurance about whether the consolidated financial statements are free of material misstatement, whether due to error or fraud. The Company is not required to have, nor were we engaged to perform, an audit of its internal control over financial reporting. As part of our audits we are required to obtain an understanding of internal control over financial reporting but not for the purpose of expressing an opinion on the effectiveness of the Company's internal control over financial reporting. Accordingly, we express no such opinion.

Our audits included performing procedures to assess the risks of material misstatement of the consolidated financial statements, whether due to error or fraud, and performing procedures that respond to those risks. Such procedures included examining, on a test basis, evidence regarding the amounts and disclosures in the consolidated financial statements. Our audits also included evaluating the accounting principles used and significant estimates made by management, as well as evaluating the overall presentation of the consolidated financial statements. We believe that our audits provide a reasonable basis for our opinion.

***Critical Audit Matters***

The critical audit matter communicated below is a matter arising from the current period audit of the consolidated financial statements that was communicated or required to be communicated to the audit committee and that (i) relates to accounts or disclosures that are material to the consolidated financial statements and (ii) involved our especially challenging, subjective, or complex judgments. The communication of critical audit matters does not alter in any way our opinion on the consolidated financial statem

tasks.description: What insights can be drawn from the 2020 corporate governance disclosures of CADIZ_INC and HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP regarding their impact on financial risks, such as asset fluctuations and revenue declines?
tasks.gold_answer: The 2020 corporate governance disclosures for CADIZ_INC and HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP provide insights into how governance practices mitigated or exacerbated financial risks. CADIZ_INC reported effective internal controls over financial reporting, which helped manage risks like the 13% decrease in other assets and significant fluctuations in accrued liabilities; for instance, the drop in accrued liabilities by 85% was strategically controlled, reducing exposure to unexpected expenses. This governance strength contributed to a more stable financial position, with consistent stock-based compensation and managed prepaid rent amortization. In contrast, HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP's governance was less transparent, with details deferred to a future proxy statement, and it faced heightened risks including a 45% revenue decline and increased net loss. However, the experienced leadership under CEO John Terwilliger facilitated risk mitigation through equity financing, which improved liquidity and reduced liabilities, though it did not address core operational issues. The comparison reveals that CADIZ_INC's explicit governance controls provided a buffer against asset and liability risks, whereas HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP's governance, while enabling financial restructuring, was insufficient to prevent revenue-related risks, underscoring the importance of comprehensive governance in stabilizing financial performance.
key_points: The answer must explicitly mention and compare the governance disclosures of both CADIZ_INC and HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP for 2020.

The answer must link the governance disclosures to specific financial risks, such as asset fluctuations and revenue declines, with concrete examples from each company (e.g., CADIZ's 13% asset decrease and 85% drop in accrued liabilities; HOUSTON_AMERICAN's 45% revenue decline).

The answer must state an insight on how governance practices mitigated or exacerbated these financial risks for each company (e.g., CADIZ's effective controls provided stability; HOUSTON_AMERICAN's governance enabled restructuring but failed on operational/revenue risks).
The answer must provide a comparative conclusion on the overall impact of governance on financial risk and stability, derived from the disclosures of both companies.

Tothe Board of Directors and Stockholders of Cadiz Inc.

***Opinion on the Financial Statements***

We have audited the accompanying consolidated balance sheets of Cadiz Inc. and its subsidiaries (the “Company”) as of December 31, 2020 and 2019, and the related consolidated statements of operations and comprehensive loss, of stockholders’ deficit and of cash flows for the years then ended, including the related notes (collectively referred to as the “consolidated financial statements”). In our opinion, the consolidated financial statements present fairly, in all material respects, the financial position of the Company as of December 31, 2020 and 2019, and the results of its operations and its cash flows for the years then ended in conformity with accounting principles generally accepted in the United States of America.

***Basis for Opinion***

These consolidated financial statements are the responsibility of the Company’s management. Our responsibility is to express an opinion on the Company’s consolidated financial statements based on our audits. We are a public accounting firm registered with the Public Company Accounting Oversight Board (United States) (PCAOB) and are required to be independent with respect to the Company in accordance with the U.S. federal securities laws and the applicable rules and regulations of the Securities and Exchange Commission and the PCAOB.

We conducted our audits of these consolidated financial statements in accordance with the standards of the PCAOB. Those standards require that we plan and perform the audit to obtain reasonable assurance about whether the consolidated financial statements are free of material misstatement, whether due to error or fraud. The Company is not required to have, nor were we engaged to perform, an audit of its internal control over financial reporting. As part of our audits we are required to obtain an understanding of internal control over financial reporting but not for the purpose of expressing an opinion on the effectiveness of the Company's internal control over financial reporting. Accordingly, we express no such opinion.

Our audits included performing procedures to assess the risks of material misstatement of the consolidated financial statements, whether due to error or fraud, and performing procedures that respond to those risks. Such procedures included examining, on a test basis, evidence regarding the amounts and disclosures in the consolidated financial statements. Our audits also included evaluating the accounting principles used and significant estimates made by management, as well as evaluating the overall presentation of the consolidated financial statements. We believe that our audits provide a reasonable basis for our opinion.

***Critical Audit Matters***

The critical audit matter communicated below is a matter arising from the current period audit of the consolidated financial statements that was communicated or required to be communicated to the audit committee and that (i) relates to accounts or disclosures that are material to the consolidated financial statements and (ii) involved our especially challenging, subjective, or complex judgments. The communication of critical audit matters does not alter in any way our opinion on the consolidated financial statem

tasks.description: In 2020, how did the types and severity of regulatory risks disclosed by CADIZ_INC and HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP differ, and what were their respective management approaches?
tasks.gold_answer: In 2020, CADIZ_INC and HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP disclosed regulatory risks with notable differences in type, severity, and management. CADIZ_INC's regulatory risks were primarily tied to obtaining approvals and permits for its water resource and land development projects, with high severity as these hurdles could prevent the company from executing its business plan and achieving profitability. Specific examples included challenges with government approvals, evidenced by lawsuits filed against the U.S. Department of Interior in March 2021. CADIZ_INC's management approach focused on navigating these regulatory processes, though the context does not detail specific mitigation strategies beyond pursuing approvals. In contrast, HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP's regulatory risks stemmed from changes in laws and regulations affecting its oil and gas operations, which could increase costs and disrupt activities. The severity was heightened by its international operations in Colombia, exposing it to additional regulatory uncertainties. HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP did not specify mitigation strategies in the context, such as compliance programs or lobbying efforts, indicating a potential lack of detailed risk management disclosure. Overall, CADIZ_INC's regulatory risks were more immediate and tied to project execution, while HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP's were broader and industry-wide, with both companies facing high-severity impacts but differing in the specificity of their disclosed management approaches.
key_points: The answer must correctly identify and differentiate the primary types of regulatory risks disclosed by each company in 2020: CADIZ_INC's risks were tied to obtaining approvals/permits for specific projects, while HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP's risks stemmed from changes in laws/regulations affecting its industry.

The answer must correctly assess and compare the severity of the disclosed risks for each company, noting that both faced high-severity impacts, but with different rationales: CADIZ_INC's risks threatened project execution and profitability, while HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP's risks involved increased costs, operational disruption, and international uncertainties.

The answer must describe the respective management approaches (or lack thereof) disclosed by each company: CADIZ_INC's approach focused on navigating regulatory processes and pursuing approvals, while HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP's disclosure lacked specific mitigation strategies.

The answer must provide a comparative synthesis, concluding that CADIZ_INC's risks were more immediate and project-specific, whereas HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP's were broader and industry-wide, and note the difference in the specificity of their disclosed management approaches.

| **Item 7A.** | **Quantitative and Qualitative Disclosures About Market Risk** |

**Commodity
Price Risk**

The
price we receive for our oil and gas production heavily influences our revenue, profitability, access to capital and future rate
of growth. Crude oil and natural gas are commodities and, therefore, their prices are subject to wide fluctuations in response
to relatively minor changes in supply and demand. Historically, the markets for oil and gas have been volatile, and these markets
will likely continue to be volatile in the future. The prices we receive for production depends on numerous factors beyond our
control.

We
have not historically entered into any hedges or other derivative commodity instruments or transactions designed to manage, or
limit exposure to oil and gas price volatility.

|  |  |
| --- | --- |

| **Item****1A.** | **Risk Factors** |

Our
business activities and the value of our securities are subject to significant hazards and risks, including those described below.
If any of such events should occur, our business, financial condition, liquidity and/or results of operations could be materially
harmed, and holders and purchasers of our securities could lose part or all of their investments.

***Company
and Organization Risks***

***We
have experienced recurring operating losses and may not attain profitability; attainment of profitability will require successful
drilling and development operations to support substantial increases in production and revenues.***

We
have incurred losses from operations in each year since 2011 and, at December 31, 2020, had an accumulated deficit of $72,021,911.
While we have implemented cost control initiatives that have brought down our cash overhead in recent years, our ability to attain
profitability is substantially dependent upon increasing our production and production revenues while continuing to control costs.
In order to increase production and revenues, we will need to successfully drill new wells on our existing, or future acquired,
acreage at a pace, and with results, significantly greater than in recent years. If, for any reason, we are unable to substantially
increase our production and revenues, while controlling drilling costs and overhead, we may never attain, or sustain, profitability.
Our ability to so increase production and revenues and attain profitability is subject to all of the other risks of oil and gas
operations as well as our ability to fund our share of drilling and development operations.

***Our
ability to operate profitably and our financial condition are highly dependent on energy prices. A substantial or extended decline
in oil and natural gas prices may adversely affect our business, financial condition or results of operations and our ability
to meet our capital expenditure obligations and financial commitments.***

The
price we receive for our oil and natural gas production heavily influences our revenue, profitability, access to capital and future
rate of growth. Oil and natural gas are commodities and, therefore, their prices are subject to wide fluctuations in response
to relatively minor changes in supply and demand. Historically, the markets for oil and natural gas have been volatile. These
markets will likely continue to be volatile in the future. The prices we receive for our production depend on numerous factors
beyond our control. These factors include, but are not limited to, the following:

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  | ● | changes in global supply and demand for oil and natural gas, including changes in demand resulting from general and specific economic conditions relating to the business cycle and other factors (e.g., global health pandemics such as COVID-19); |
|  |  |  |
|  | ● | the actions of the Organization of Petroleum Exporting Countries, or OPEC; |
|  |  |  |
|  | ● | the price and quantity of imports of foreign oil and natural gas; |
|  |  |  |
|  | ● | political conditions, including embargoes, in or affecting other oil-producing activity; |
|  |  |  |
|  | ● | the level of global oil and natural gas exploration and production activity; |
|  |  |  |
|  | ● | the level of global oil and natural gas inventories; |
|  |  |  |
|  | ● | weather conditions; |
|  |  |  |
|  | ● | technological advances affecting energy consumption, including renewable energy initiatives that result in energy consumption transitioning away from fossil fuels; and |
|  |  |  |
|  | ● | the price and availability of alternative fuels. |

Global
economic growth drives demand for energy from all sources, including fossil fuels. Should the U.S. and global economies experience
weakness, demand for energy may decline. Similarly, should growth in global energy production outstrip demand, excess supplies
may arise. Declines in demand and excess supplies may result in accompanying declines in commodity prices and deterioration of
our financial position along with our ability to operate profitably and our ability to obtain financing to support operations.

|  |
| --- |
| 14 |

|  |
| --- |
|  |

With
respect to our business, we have experienced periodic declines in demand thought to be associated with slowing economic growth
in certain markets, including the effects of the COVID-19 pandemic, coupled with new oil and gas supplies coming on line and other
circumstances beyond our control that resulted in oil and gas supply exceeding global demand which, in turn, resulted in steep
declines in prices of oil and natural gas. There can be no assurance as to how low the current price decline will persist or that
a reoccurrence of price weakness will not arise in the future.

Past
declines in prices reduced, and any declines that may occur in the future can be expected to reduce, our revenues and profitability

and natural gas that we can produce economically, resulting in def

tasks.description: Compare the financial risks, including indebtedness and financing needs, for CADIZ_INC and HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP in 2020, and analyze their implications for risk severity and mitigation strategies.
tasks.gold_answer: In 2020, CADIZ_INC and HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP faced distinct financial risks related to indebtedness and financing needs, with varying severity and mitigation approaches. CADIZ_INC reported significant indebtedness of approximately $78.6 million, secured by its assets, and a clear need for additional working capital to fund operations until its development programs generate sufficient revenues. This created high-severity risks, as the company had not generated significant revenues and relied on successful asset development to become profitable. The context indicates that CADIZ_INC's mitigation strategy involved seeking additional financing, but it did not specify details such as debt restructuring or equity raises, highlighting uncertainty in meeting financial obligations. In contrast, HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP's financial risks were primarily driven by commodity price volatility, as its revenue and profitability depended on crude oil and natural gas prices, which are subject to wide fluctuations. While the context does not explicitly mention indebtedness or specific financing needs for HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP, the absence of hedging or derivative instruments to manage price risk suggests a high-severity exposure to market conditions without proactive mitigation. Comparatively, CADIZ_INC's financial risks were more directly tied to liquidity and debt burdens, with mitigation focused on operational success, whereas HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP's risks were market-driven with no disclosed financial risk management strategies, indicating a deeper vulnerability to external economic factors.
key_points: The answer must explicitly compare the indebtedness of both companies, stating CADIZ_INC's specific debt level and acknowledging the absence of explicit indebtedness information for HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP.

The answer must explicitly compare the financing needs of both companies, stating CADIZ_INC's need for working capital and acknowledging the absence of explicit financing needs information for HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP.

The answer must analyze the implications for risk severity for each company, distinguishing between CADIZ_INC's high-severity liquidity/debt risks and HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP's high-severity market price risks.

The answer must analyze the mitigation strategies (or lack thereof) for each company, mentioning CADIZ_INC's strategy of seeking financing and HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP's lack of disclosed hedging strategies.

tasks.description: How did the litigation and operational risks disclosed by CADIZ_INC and HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP in 2020 differ in terms of type, potential impact, and the companies' approaches to risk management?
tasks.gold_answer: In 2020, CADIZ_INC and HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP disclosed litigation and operational risks with significant differences in type, potential impact, and management approaches. CADIZ_INC faced litigation risks primarily from lawsuits challenging government approvals related to its property, such as those filed in March 2021 against the U.S. Department of Interior. These risks had high potential impact, as they could delay or prevent the company from executing its business plan and developing land assets, directly threatening its path to profitability. CADIZ_INC's management approach involved addressing these legal challenges, though the context does not specify strategies like settlement or legal defenses. In terms of operational risks, CADIZ_INC's were implied through regulatory hurdles and development challenges, but not explicitly detailed like accidents or failures. Conversely, HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP's operational risks included accidents, equipment failures, and natural disasters that could impact production and cause significant losses, with high potential impact due to the capital-intensive nature of oil and gas operations. The context does not mention litigation risks for HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP, focusing instead on operational and international risks. HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP did not disclose specific mitigation for operational risks, such as safety protocols or insurance, indicating a potential gap in risk management disclosure. Overall, CADIZ_INC's risks were more legally oriented with direct business plan implications, while HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP's were operationally focused with broader industry threats, and both companies showed limited detail in their risk management approaches, suggesting areas for improved transparency.
key_points: The answer must explicitly state the primary type of disclosed risk for each company: litigation risks for CADIZ_INC and operational risks for HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP.
The answer must describe the potential impact of the disclosed risks for each company, such as business plan delays for CADIZ_INC and production/property losses for HOUSTON_AMERICAN_ENERGY_CORP.
The answer must describe or characterize each company's disclosed approach to risk management, noting any limitations or lack of detail in their disclosures.
The answer must provide a comparative analysis, highlighting the core difference in risk focus (legal vs. operational) between the two companies.

While we have implemented cost control initiatives that have brought down our cash overhead in recent years, our ability to attain
profitability is substantially dependent upon increasing our production and production revenues while continuing to control costs.
In order to increase production and revenues, we will need to successfully drill new wells on our existing, or future acquired,
acreage at a pace, and with results, significantly greater than in recent years. If, for any reason, we are unable to substantially
increase our production and revenues, while controlling drilling costs and overhead, we may never attain, or sustain, profitability.
Our ability to so increase production and revenues and attain profitability is subject to all of the other risks of oil and gas
operations as well as our ability to fund our share of drilling and development operations.

***Our
ability to operate profitably and our financial condition are highly dependent on energy prices. A substantial or extended decline
in oil and natural gas prices may adversely affect our business, financial condition or results of operations and our ability
to meet our capital expenditure obligations and financial commitments.***

price we receive for our oil and natural gas production heavily influences our revenue, profitability, access to capital and future
rate of growth. Oil and natural gas are commodities and, therefore, their prices are subject to wide fluctuations in response
to relatively minor changes in supply and demand. Historically, the markets for oil and natural gas have been volatile. These
markets will likely continue to be volatile in the future. The prices we receive for our production depend on numerous factors
beyond our control. These factors include, but are not limited to, the following:

|  | ● | technological advances affecting energy consumption, including renewable energy initiatives that result in energy consumption transitioning away from fossil fuels; and |

|  | ● | the price and availability of alternative fuels. |

economic growth drives demand for energy from all sources, including fossil fuels. Should the U.S. and global economies experience
weakness, demand for energy may decline. Similarly, should growth in global energy production outstrip demand, excess supplies
may arise. Declines in demand and excess supplies may result in accompanying declines in commodity prices and deterioration of
our financial position along with our ability to operate profitably and our ability to obtain financing to support operations.
